source.title: Effect of dentate gyrus disruption on remembering what happened where.

confidence_rationale: Causal lesion-style evidence (Bax-KO mice with disrupted dentate gyrus circuitry) showing the DG is required for binding internal spatial map with external object/event information when memory must persist across an interrupted delay. Online maintenance is intact; episodic encoding requiring spatial-nonspatial binding is impaired. Direct evidence for the hippocampus (specifically DG) as the convergence locus where z_resource-equivalent (objects/events) and z_world-equivalent (spatial map) are integrated. Confidence moderate because a single-mechanism mouse study and one paradigm.
failure_signatures: The Bax-KO model disrupts DG by disrupting newborn-granule-cell turnover, which has effects beyond pure DG function (it changes adult neurogenesis, which affects pattern separation specifically). The paradigm is a delayed-non-match-to-place task, which is a narrow operationalisation of episodic binding. If REE's z_resource/z_world fusion happens at a hippocampal stage downstream of DG (e.g., CA3 or CA1), the DG-specific lesion may not be the right model for the convergence-locus claim.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In Bax-knockout mice with selectively disrupted dentate gyrus circuitry, online working memory across short delays (up to 5 min) is intact, but episodic memory requiring binding of internal spatial map with external object/event information across an interrupted delay (65 s, mouse removed from maze) is profoundly impaired. The DG is required for spatial-nonspatial binding in the formation of episodic memory.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-030 axis 3 (hippocampal planning: fused input or parallel streams?) gets direct causal evidence here. The mammalian substrate uses fusion at a specific hippocampal subregion: the dentate gyrus is required for binding spatial map (z_world-equivalent) with object/event (z_resource-equivalent) information. This supports MECH-162 (z_resource and z_world re-converge at hippocampal planning) and provides a specific anatomical anchor: the convergence is not generic 'hippocampal,' it is DG-mediated. For REE, this informs the substrate design: the z_resource/z_world fusion locus should be the analogue of DG (a sparse pattern-separating layer), not a generic associative network. It also informs axis 5 feedback: since DG receives both spatial input (entorhinal medial path) and object input (entorhinal lateral path), the convergence is feedforward, not feedback.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Bax-KO mouse model disrupts DG circuitry by accumulating ungroomed newborn granule cells -- this is one of many possible DG disruption methods and primarily affects pattern separation rather than DG function broadly. The paradigm (delayed-non-match-to-place) is one operationalisation of episodic binding. Generalising 'DG required for spatial-object binding' to 'REE z_resource/z_world fusion locus must be DG-like' is an analogy of function. The exact mechanism (sparse coding, pattern separation, mossy-fibre input specificity) is not characterised by the behavioural endpoint alone.
mapping.source_context: Bax-knockout mice (genetic disruption of newborn granule cell apoptosis); delayed-non-match-to-place task; comparison with wild-type mice; single behavioural paradigm with online vs interrupted delay manipulation.

confidence_components.notes: Source_quality moderate-high (Frontiers behavioural, single-paradigm study). Mapping_fidelity good because the paper directly tests the binding-locus question Q-030 axis 3 asks. Transfer_risk elevated because mouse model and DG-specific lesion may not generalise to the broader hippocampal convergence question.
